seasonAlert.title: High downy mildew risk
seasonAlert.message: Rain and heat expected this week. Keep an eye on your leaves.

mildiou.name: Downy mildew
mildiou.description: Downy mildew is caused by the fungus Plasmopara viticola. It attacks all green parts of the vine, mainly the leaves.
mildiou.symptom1: Oily yellow spots on the upper surface of leaves
mildiou.symptom2: White cottony down on the underside
mildiou.treatment: Preventive copper-based treatment (Bordeaux mixture). Apply before rain, renew every 10-14 days.
mildiou.season: May to August — favored by heat and humidity
mildiou.preventive2: Improve air circulation through shoot thinning
mildiou.spread: Spores dispersed by wind and rain splash

oidium.name: Powdery mildew
oidium.description: Powdery mildew is caused by Erysiphe necator. It develops in warm, dry weather, unlike downy mildew.
oidium.symptom1: White-grey powder on leaves and clusters
oidium.symptom2: Berries that crack or dry out
oidium.treatment: Sulfur dusting or spraying. Preventive treatments from bud break.
oidium.season: April to September — favored by warm, dry weather

blackRot.name: Black rot
blackRot.description: Black rot is caused by Guignardia bidwellii. It causes significant damage to berries.
blackRot.symptom1: Circular brown spots bordered with black on leaves
blackRot.treatment: Remove mummified berries. Preventive fungicide treatments in spring.
blackRot.season: May to July — favored by spring rains
blackRot.condition3: Presence of mummified berries from the previous year
blackRot.spread: Spores released from mummies by rain

esca.name: Esca
esca.description: Esca is a complex of wood diseases caused by several fungi. A chronic disease that can kill the vine.
esca.symptom1: Discoloration between leaf veins (tiger stripe pattern)
esca.treatment: No curative treatment. Cutting back affected vine. Protect pruning wounds.
esca.season: Symptoms visible in summer — June to September
esca.preventive1: Protect pruning wounds with sealant paste
esca.curative3: Trunk renewal if the vine is not too affected

botrytis.name: Botrytis (Grey mold)
botrytis.description: Grey mold is caused by Botrytis cinerea. It attacks clusters at maturity.
botrytis.treatment: Promote cluster aeration. Leaf removal. Anti-botrytis treatments before cluster closure.
botrytis.season: August to harvest — favored by humidity
botrytis.spread: Airborne spores, favored by berry wounds

flavescence.name: Flavescence dorée
flavescence.description: Phytoplasma disease transmitted by the leafhopper Scaphoideus titanus. Regulated disease, mandatory reporting.
flavescence.symptom1: Leaf rolling with yellow or red coloration depending on variety
flavescence.treatment: Mandatory uprooting of contaminated vines. Insecticide treatment against the vector leafhopper.
flavescence.condition1: Presence of the leafhopper Scaphoideus titanus
flavescence.condition2: Vineyards not treated against the vector
flavescence.preventive1: Mandatory insecticide treatment against the leafhopper
flavescence.preventive2: Prospection and uprooting of affected vines
flavescence.spread: Transmitted by the leafhopper Scaphoideus titanus

leafBlight.name: Leaf Blight
leafBlight.description: Leaf blight (Isariopsis Leaf Spot) is caused by the fungus Pseudocercospora vitis. It produces angular reddish-brown spots delimited by leaf veins.
leafBlight.symptom1: Angular reddish-brown spots delimited by veins
leafBlight.treatment: Preventive copper or mancozeb-based fungicide treatment. Remove fallen leaves in autumn.
leafBlight.spread: Spores spread by rain and wind

chlorose.name: Iron chlorosis
chlorose.description: Leaf yellowing due to iron deficiency, often linked to overly calcareous soil.
chlorose.symptom1: Yellowing between veins, veins remaining green
chlorose.treatment: Iron chelate application. Choose rootstock adapted to calcareous soils.
chlorose.season: Spring — especially on calcareous soils after heavy rain
chlorose.preventive1: Choose rootstock adapted to calcareous soils
chlorose.spread: Not contagious — nutritional deficiency linked to soil

colorTexture.title: Color and texture
colorTexture.body: A healthy vine leaf shows a uniform, vibrant and bright green. The upper surface is smooth while the underside is slightly downy. Veins are clear, well-defined and a slightly lighter green than the blade. Run your finger along the leaf: it should feel firm, supple and free of abnormal roughness.
colorTexture.tip: A healthy leaf never has brown, yellow or powdery spots. If you see any, scan it immediately with VinEye.

shape.title: Shape and symmetry
shape.body: Leaf shape varies by variety: 3 lobes (Merlot), 5 lobes (Cabernet Sauvignon) or nearly entire (Gamay). Regardless of variety, a healthy leaf is symmetrical with regular serrated edges. The sinuses (indentations between lobes) are clean. An asymmetric or deformed leaf may indicate a viral issue or herbicide damage.

warning.title: When to worry
warning.body: Watch for these early signs: discoloration between veins (chlorosis), translucent oily spots (downy mildew), white powder (powdery mildew), downward leaf edge rolling (water stress or viral), brown necrosis (black rot). The earlier you act, the more effective the treatment.
warning.tip: Photograph the suspicious leaf with VinEye at the first symptoms. Early detection is the key to successful treatment.

winter.title: Winter (December-February)
winter.body: This is the dormant period. Use this time to prune the vine, remove and burn dead wood (a source of inoculum). Apply a winter treatment with dormant oil to eliminate scale insect eggs and overwintering pest forms. Clean pruning tools between each vine to prevent esca spread.

spring.title: Spring (March-May)
spring.body: Bud break marks the beginning of the vigilance season. From the 2-3 unfolded leaves stage, begin preventive treatments: Bordeaux mixture against downy mildew, sulfur against powdery mildew. Renew after each rainfall exceeding 10 mm. Watch for chlorosis on calcareous soils. This is also the time to treat against the leafhopper vector of flavescence dorée (mandatory treatment in regulated zones).
spring.tip: The first preventive treatment must be applied at the 2-3 unfolded leaves stage. Don't miss it — it's the most important of the season.

summer.title: Summer (June-August)
summer.body: Active monitoring period. Downy mildew peaks in June, powdery mildew in July. Adapt your treatments to the weather: increase after rain for downy mildew, during heat waves for powdery mildew. Practice leaf removal to ventilate clusters and reduce botrytis risk. Watch for esca symptoms (tiger-striped leaves, sudden apoplexy).
summer.tip: After each rainfall over 10 mm, inspect your vines within 48 hours. This is when downy mildew infections occur.

autumn.title: Autumn (September-November)
autumn.body: Harvest time and final treatments. Watch for botrytis on ripe clusters, especially if humidity is high. After harvest, a final copper treatment can protect remaining foliage and reduce inoculum for the following year. Prepare for winter by collecting fallen leaves and plant debris.

reds.title: Iconic red varieties
reds.body: Merlot is the most planted red grape in Bordeaux. Soft and fruity, it dominates the right bank (Saint-Émilion, Pomerol). It is susceptible to downy mildew and botrytis. Cabernet Sauvignon reigns on the left bank (Médoc, Graves). More tannic and structured, it resists diseases better but is susceptible to powdery mildew. Cabernet Franc, the third Bordeaux variety, offers aromas of bell pepper and violet. It is more resistant to grey rot than Merlot.

whites.title: White varieties
whites.body: Sauvignon Blanc brings freshness and citrus aromas. A vigorous variety, it requires careful leaf removal to avoid botrytis. Sémillon is the great variety of Sauternes sweet wines. Paradoxically, its susceptibility to 'noble rot' (Botrytis cinerea under controlled conditions) is what makes these wines great. Muscadelle, rarer, completes the blend with its floral notes.

choosing.title: Choosing your variety
choosing.body: The choice of variety depends on the terroir: Merlot prefers cool clay soils, Cabernet Sauvignon well-drained gravel soils. Climate matters too: late-ripening varieties like Cabernet Sauvignon need heat to ripen. Also consider disease susceptibility: in humid areas, favor varieties resistant to downy mildew.
choosing.tip: Merlot is more tolerant of clay and cool soils, while Cabernet Sauvignon prefers well-drained, warm gravel. Match your choice to your terroir.
